threats.title: Unauthenticated Guest Contract Allows Complete Drain of Accidentally Sent Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: The Guest contract is deployed as a singleton and contains a fallback function that executes arbitrary calls via LibOptim.call without any authentication or msg.sender checks. If users accidentally send ETH or ERC20 tokens to the Guest contract address, any external actor can immediately drain these funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Public good/singleton contracts that lack authentication on execution functions are prime targets for MEV bots. Confirming that the Guest contract can hold and transfer funds without reverting is critical for assessing honeypot risks.
threats.config_evidence: Deploy.s.sol deploys Guest as a standalone contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Fallback function decodes payload and calls _dispatchGuest without authentication

preconditions: Guest contract holds ETH or ERC20 tokens (e.g., via accidental user transfer)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: LibOptim.call(call.to, call.value, ..., call.data) executes with no access control
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker constructs a payload with call.to = token, call.data = transfer(attacker, balance)
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker extracts 100% of any funds accidentally sent to the Guest contract

economic_questions: Can the Guest contract receive ETH directly?
Are there any protocol integrations that whitelist the Guest contract, allowing bypass of msg.sender checks?
validation_requirements: Test sending ETH to Guest and extracting it via fallback
Test extracting ERC20 tokens from Guest via fallback

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 100% of funds held by Guest contract

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after a user accidentally sends funds to the Guest contract
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Direct loss to the user who made the mistake

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users who mistakenly send funds to the Guest contract address

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if Guest has a receive() function or if fallback handles empty calldata
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify LibOptim.call behavior with attacker-controlled parameters

threats.title: Zero-Address Signer Configuration Allows Complete Wallet Takeover via Invalid Signatures
threats.hypothesis_description: In BaseSig.sol, ecrecover returns address(0) when signature validation fails. The contract does not check for address(0) and proceeds to add the configured weight for address(0) to the total signature weight. If a wallet's configuration accidentally includes address(0) as a signer, any attacker can submit an invalid signature to completely take over the wallet.
threats.investigation_rationale: Failing open on ecrecover is a classic vulnerability. While it requires a specific misconfiguration (address(0) in the Merkle tree), the impact is a complete, unauthenticated wallet drain.
deployment_assumptions: Wallet configuration (imageHash) includes address(0) as a leaf with non-zero weight
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence prevents this; relies on off-chain SDK correctness.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ecrecover return value is used without checking for address(0)

preconditions: Wallet imageHash includes address(0) as a signer

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker provides v=0, r=0, s=0, causing ecrecover to return address(0)
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker gains full authorization to execute arbitrary calls, draining all wallet assets

economic_questions: How likely is the off-chain SDK to generate a configuration with address(0)?
Can an attacker trick a user into signing a configuration update that includes address(0)?
validation_requirements: Deploy wallet with address(0) in config
Attempt execution with empty/invalid signature bytes

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after vulnerable configuration is set

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify ecrecover behavior with malformed compact signatures

threats.title: Relayer Gas Manipulation Allows Selective Call Griefing in Batches
threats.hypothesis_description: The Calls.execute function allows users to specify a gasLimit for individual calls. A relayer can manipulate the transaction's overall gas limit to provide exactly enough gas to pass the gasleft() < gasLimit check, but not enough for the call to succeed. If the call uses BEHAVIOR_IGNORE_ERROR, it fails silently while the rest of the batch (including relayer fee payments) succeeds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Relayers have control over the exact gas provided to a transaction. If they can force a user's primary action to fail while still collecting their fee, it creates a misaligned economic incentive.

proof_of_signal.pattern: gasleft() < gasLimit check followed by LibOptim.call which can fail due to 63/64 gas rule

preconditions: User submits a batch via a relayer
Batch includes a fee payment call and a target call with BEHAVIOR_IGNORE_ERROR
Target call has a specific gasLimit set

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Relayer restricts outer gas so gasleft() >= gasLimit passes, but the 63/64th gas forwarded is insufficient for the sub-call
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Relayer collects fee without executing the user's expensive computation, saving gas costs and profiting the difference

economic_questions: Do users commonly use BEHAVIOR_IGNORE_ERROR for critical calls?
What is the exact gas margin a relayer can exploit using the 63/64 rule?
validation_requirements: Construct a payload with a fee transfer and a high-gas target call
Execute via relayer with precisely tuned gas limit to force target call failure

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Relayer pays base gas, saves on sub-call gas
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Difference between fee collected and actual gas spent

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: High gas price environments where saving computation is highly profitable
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Degraded user experience and minor financial loss (fees paid for failed actions)

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users submitting transactions via the malicious relayer
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of transaction fee without intended state change

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Analyze 63/64 gas forwarding mechanics in LibOptim.call
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Calculate exact gas thresholds required for the exploit

threats.title: Uncancelable Recovery Payloads Force High-Stakes Race Condition
threats.hypothesis_description: Once a recovery payload is queued via queuePayload, it cannot be canceled. If a recovery signer's key is compromised, the attacker can queue a wallet takeover payload. The legitimate owner's only defense is to completely update the wallet's configuration before the requiredDeltaTime timelock expires, creating a race condition.
threats.investigation_rationale: The lack of a direct cancellation mechanism for queued recovery payloads places immense operational burden on the wallet owner during a compromise event, increasing the likelihood of a successful takeover.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Payload is added to timestampForQueuedPayload with no corresponding remove/cancel function

preconditions: Attacker queues a malicious configuration update payload

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: timestampForQueuedPayload is write-once and never cleared
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker waits out the requiredDeltaTime, then executes the payload
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker gains full control of the wallet and drains all assets if the owner fails to update the imageHash in time

economic_questions: What is the typical requiredDeltaTime configured by users?
How quickly can a user realistically execute an updateImageHash transaction during a compromise?
validation_requirements: Verify that updating the wallet imageHash effectively invalidates the queued recovery payload
Test the exact timing mechanics of the timelock expiration

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Queueing during periods when the owner is unlikely to notice (e.g., weekends)

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Wallet owners with compromised recovery signers
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets in the affected wallet

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Analyze interaction between BaseSig imageHash validation and Recovery root return
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm that changing imageHash breaks the recovery signature chain

threats.title: Session Usage Limit Wasting via IGNORE_ERROR Behavior
threats.hypothesis_description: Session keys are subject to cumulative usage limits. These limits are incremented based on the payload's intended actions before execution begins. If a session key submits a payload where calls use BEHAVIOR_IGNORE_ERROR and those calls fail, the usage limits are still permanently consumed, allowing a relayer to drain a session key's limits without executing actions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The separation of limit incrementing (first call) and actual execution (subsequent calls) means failures in execution still cost the user their limits. This can be exploited for griefing.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Usage limits are validated and incremented via the first call, regardless of subsequent call success

preconditions: Session key has strict usage limits
Session key submits a batch with BEHAVIOR_IGNORE_ERROR calls
Relayer or external conditions cause the calls to fail

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Limits incremented before execution; no refund mechanism if execution fails
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Relayer manipulates gas to fail the call, consuming the session key's limits
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit for attacker, but causes DoS for the session key holder, potentially preventing timely actions (e.g., collateral top-ups)

economic_questions: Can this be used to prevent a session key from executing a critical time-sensitive action?
Is there any way for the session key to recover lost limits?
validation_requirements: Execute a payload where the first call (increment) succeeds but subsequent calls fail
Verify that limits are permanently consumed

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas cost of the failed transaction
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect (e.g., profiting from a liquidation the session key couldn't prevent)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When the session key needs to execute a critical action near their limit boundary

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of session privileges until limits are reset by owner
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Value dependent on the prevented action

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm state changes persist when IGNORE_ERROR calls fail

threats.title: Out-of-Bounds Calldata Read in Permission Validator Bypasses Zero-Value Checks
threats.hypothesis_description: PermissionValidator uses LibBytes.readBytes32 to read parameters from call.data. This function does not perform bounds checking and silently returns 0 for out-of-bounds reads. If a permission rule enforces that a parameter must be 0, an attacker can provide truncated calldata to pass the check. If the target contract uses msg.data.length for dynamic logic instead of standard ABI decoding, this could bypass intended restrictions.
threats.investigation_rationale: Silent zero-returns on out-of-bounds reads create a discrepancy between what the validator sees (0) and what the target contract sees (truncated data). While standard ABI decoding also pads with zeros, custom fallback functions might behave differently.

proof_of_signal.pattern: calldataload is used without checking if _pointer + 32 <= _data.length

preconditions: Session key has a rule checking a parameter against 0
Target contract implements custom calldata parsing that behaves differently on truncated data vs zero-padded data

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: readBytes32 returns 0 for out-of-bounds, satisfying EQUAL 0 or LESS_THAN_OR_EQUAL 0 rules
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker sends truncated call.data, passing the permission check
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker executes an unintended action on the target contract if it misinterprets the truncated calldata

economic_questions: Are there common DeFi protocols that use custom calldata parsing vulnerable to this?
Can this be used to bypass array length checks?
validation_requirements: Construct a payload with truncated calldata targeting a custom fallback contract
Verify permission validation passes and target contract executes

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends entirely on the target contract's vulnerability

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Wallet owners using session keys with specific target contracts

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test EVM calldataload behavior on short slices
